text: During the incident, p99 latency on the cart service spiked to 1.4 seconds. Initial inspection blamed Redis because GET calls were slow. Root cause was actually downstream: the application's connection pool to Redis was saturated under load, so most calls were waiting for a free connection rather than executing. Increasing pool size and adding a circuit breaker resolved the issue. Redis itself was healthy throughout.

Lock contention on a Redis key used as a distributed mutex caused thread-pool exhaustion in the order service. Threads acquired the mutex serially, queued behind a slow holder, and the upstream API timed out. Fix was to shorten lock hold time and shard the mutex by tenant. The Redis span itself reported normal latency per call; the pathology only appeared in trace shape, not in single-span timing.

Injected fault: replication lag on the primary read replica caused stale reads in the catalog service. Symptoms presented as intermittent 500ms-2s latency on read queries, with no error responses. The slow span is the database read, but the cause is replica health, not query plan. Verification requires comparing replica lag metrics against the trace timestamp.

CPU throttling on a containerized recommendation service caused 800ms-1500ms latency spikes that propagated up through the social-network call graph. The throttled span itself appeared as a single slow leaf, but every parent in the chain reported similar wall-time inflation. Pattern: many spans inflated by a similar additive amount points to a shared-resource bottleneck rather than a per-span issue.

An API gateway timeout was misconfigured to 30 seconds while the downstream service had a 5-second SLO. When the downstream slowed under partial outage, the gateway held connections open for the full 30s, exhausting its own connection budget and causing cascading 503s on unrelated endpoints. The visible symptom was gateway errors; the real cause was a config mismatch between two layers.

Kafka consumer lag built up due to a slow downstream sink. The consumer span looked healthy in the trace, but the producer side experienced backpressure that surfaced as elevated checkout latency. Kafka itself was not the bottleneck; the sink was. Trace-level evidence required walking from the producer through the broker to the consumer's downstream call.

A long-running analytical query held a row-level lock on the orders table. Concurrent OLTP writes blocked behind it, manifesting as elevated latency on order-create spans across the fleet. The slow span is the order-create insert; the cause is an unrelated query in another service holding the lock. Cross-service evidence is required to identify the holder.

Memory pressure on the Memcached layer triggered eviction storms during peak traffic. Hit rate dropped from 98 percent to 70 percent; the resulting database fan-out caused order-service latency to triple. The slow span looks like the database, but the upstream cause is cache eviction. Hit-rate metrics alongside trace data are required to confirm.

Third-party payment provider degradation caused intermittent 3-8 second latency on checkout. The slow span is the outbound HTTP call to the provider. Diagnosis is straightforward once the provider is identified, but the agent must correctly attribute the latency to an external dependency rather than internal logic. Status-page lookup and historical comparison disambiguate.

An incorrect load balancer health check threshold removed healthy backends from rotation during a brief latency blip, concentrating traffic on the remaining nodes and triggering a real overload. The original blip was self-healing; the health-check misconfiguration turned it into a full incident. Trace evidence shows latency cliffs aligned with backend pool size changes.

Thread-pool exhaustion in a tier-1 service caused requests to queue inside the JVM before any downstream call was made. The trace shows the parent span as long but with a small number of child calls of normal duration; the gap between span start and first child is the queueing time. This is the signature pattern for in-process queueing rather than downstream slowness.

A misconfigured retry policy in the service mesh caused a single failed downstream call to retry up to 8 times with exponential backoff. The aggregate trace span reflected total elapsed time across retries rather than a single slow call. The fix was to disable retries on non-idempotent endpoints; verification required counting retry attempts from mesh-level metadata.
